title: Keeping Student Health and Personal Info Safe

pages.title: Welcome to Keeping Student Health and Personal Info Safe
pages.subtitle: Learn how to protect student records, health-related information, and personal data in school settings.

pages.title: Why student privacy matters
pages.text: Schools handle sensitive information every day. This may include student names, addresses, parent contact details, grades, schedules, disability accommodations, and health-related information. Protecting this information helps keep students safe, preserves trust, and supports compliance with privacy requirements such as FERPA.

pages.title: What FERPA protects
pages.text:
FERPA, the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act, helps protect the privacy of student education records. In a school setting, this can include information kept by the school that directly relates to a student. Examples may include:
  • Student identification details
  • Grades and academic records
  • Attendance records
  • Parent or guardian contact information
  • Some records related to student support, accommodations, or health information kept by the school

pages.title: Examples of sensitive student information
pages.text:
Sensitive student information can appear in many everyday situations. Examples include:
  • A class list with names, addresses, and parent phone numbers
  • A doctor's note describing a student's medical condition
  • Accommodation information for classroom support
  • Behavior, discipline, or counseling-related records
  • Emails discussing a student's personal situation
Even when the goal is to help the student, this information should only be shared with authorized people and through approved channels.

pages.title: Only share with authorized people
pages.text:
Student information should only be accessed or shared by people who are authorized and have a legitimate school-related need to know. This does not mean it should be casually shared with:
  • Other staff who do not need the information
  • Visitors
  • Friends or family
  • Public forums or online communities
  • External AI tools without school approval and proper safeguards
When in doubt, follow school policy and ask the appropriate administrator before sharing.

pages.title: Digital sharing risks
pages.text:
Digital tools make it easy to accidentally expose student information. Examples of unsafe behavior include:
  • Copying identifiable student information into a public forum
  • Pasting student details into an external AI tool without authorization
  • Forwarding student records through personal email or messaging apps
  • Leaving sensitive files open on a shared or unlocked device
Convenience should never override student privacy requirements.

image.alt: Digital sharing risks for student information

pages.title: Paper records and physical privacy
pages.text:
Student privacy is not only a digital issue. Printed records should also be protected. Examples of safer handling include:
  • Putting student lists away when not in use
  • Using locked drawers, cabinets, or folders
  • Not leaving records face-up on desks, printers, or copiers
  • Avoiding taking sensitive student records home unless school policy allows it

pages.title: Social media, chats, and casual sharing
pages.text: It may feel harmless to post about a class activity or mention a student casually, but these actions can still create privacy risks. Photos, names, health details, and personal situations should not be shared on personal social media, informal staff chats, or other unapproved channels unless school policy and permissions clearly allow it. Even partial information can reveal more than intended.

image.alt: Risks of social media and informal sharing

pages.title: School devices and account access
pages.text:
School devices and school accounts may provide access to student records, email, and internal systems.

Do not allow unauthorized people to use a device while you are signed in.

Do not share your credentials, and do not assume brief access is harmless.

If someone needs access, direct them through proper school administration or IT procedures.

image.alt: Protecting school devices and signed-in accounts

pages.title: What to do if student information is exposed
pages.text:
If you think student information was shared or exposed improperly:
  1. Stop the exposure if possible
  2. Do not try to hide the mistake
  3. Report the incident immediately according to school policy
  4. Notify the appropriate administrator, privacy contact, or designated school official
Quick reporting helps the school assess the situation and reduce harm.

pages.title: Ready for the quiz?
pages.subtitle: This short quiz will test your understanding of FERPA awareness and safe handling of student information.
